description: The Southern Birdwing (Troides minos) is a stunning swallowtail butterfly endemic to southern India. It holds the title of being India's second-largest butterfly, boasting an impressive wingspan of 140 to 190 mm. It was widely considered the largest until 2020 when the Golden Birdwing officially claimed the top spot. The male features striking black forewings and bright golden-yellow hindwings with sharp black borders, while the slightly larger female exhibits more extensive black patterns across her wings. This vivid yellow and black coloration is not just for show; it serves as a vital aposematic warning. It visually alerts predators to the butterfly's toxicity, which is acquired from the aristolochic acid ingested by the caterpillar during its larval stage. These butterflies are strong, gliding fliers, often observed sailing high above the forest canopy or rapidly swooping down to feed on nectar-rich flowers in the early morning. As the official state butterfly of Karnataka, it is highly prized by naturalists and remains a major highlight for enthusiasts exploring the rich biodiversity of the Western Ghats.
habitat: Found across diverse habitats including lowland evergreen forests, mixed deciduous forests, dry scrublands, and agricultural fields, primarily localized within the Western Ghats and parts of the Eastern Ghats at altitudes up to 910 meters.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from flowers like Lantana, Mussaenda, and Ixora. Caterpillars feed exclusively on the leaves of Aristolochia plant species.
life_span: The adult butterfly typically lives for 2 to 3 weeks, completing its entire four-stage life cycle in just over a month.

description: The Blue Mormon (Papilio polymnestor) is a large and striking swallowtail butterfly endemic to southern India and Sri Lanka. With an impressive wingspan of 120 to 150 mm, it ranks as one of the largest butterflies in India and is proudly recognized as the state butterfly of Maharashtra. The male’s wings are a rich, velvety black, highlighted by pale, shimmering blue or greyish-blue streaks on the forewings. Its hindwings are dominantly pale blue with an intricate pattern of black discal and marginal spots, notably lacking the characteristic "tails" seen in many swallowtails. The underside of both wings features distinct bright red spots near the base. Females exhibit similar but slightly duller and paler coloration. A strong and restless flier, the Blue Mormon is frequently observed rapidly hopping up and down along forest paths. It is known to actively engage in mud-puddling to absorb essential minerals and is often seen basking in the sun or swiftly moving between flowers to feed on nectar. Though typically a forest dweller, its adaptability to cultivated citrus plants allows it to thrive even in wooded urban environments.
habitat: Primarily frequents heavy rainfall areas such as evergreen and riparian forests. However, it is highly adaptable and can also be found in mixed deciduous forests, lush home gardens, and wooded urban areas, largely due to the cultivation of its citrus host plants.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from flowers such as Mussaenda, Ixora, Jasminum, and Hibiscus. Caterpillars feed primarily on the leaves of the Rutaceae (citrus) family, including Citrus grandis, Citrus reticulata, and wild species like Atalantia racemosa.
life_span: The complete developmental life cycle from egg to adult takes approximately 40 to 46 days. The adult butterfly typically lives for about 2 to 3 weeks in the wild.
conservation_status: Common (Not Evaluated on the global IUCN Red List)

description: The Malabar Banded Peacock (Papilio buddha) is a spectacularly beautiful swallowtail butterfly endemic to the Western Ghats of India. Highly prized for its striking appearance, it boasts a wingspan of 90 to 100 mm. Its wings present a dramatic visual contrast: the upper sides are predominantly black, bisected by a magnificent, broad band of iridescent emerald or bluish-green that shimmers brightly when the butterfly is in flight. The hindwings feature distinct spatulate tails and a row of sub-marginal green spots, while the undersides are muted with dark brown, black, and pale lunules to provide camouflage when resting. Revered for its beauty, it was officially declared the state butterfly of Kerala in 2018. It is a forest-dwelling species that is a fast, powerful flier, often found near streams or wet areas within dense canopy forests. Although locally common in specific protected pockets, it is under pressure due to habitat loss affecting its specific larval host plants, making it a species of high conservation significance.
habitat: Confined to the tropical evergreen and semi-evergreen forests of the Western Ghats, particularly at mid-elevations where there is dense canopy cover and high humidity.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from flowers such as Lantana, Ixora, and Clerodendrum. Caterpillars feed exclusively on the leaves of plants in the citrus family (Rutaceae), with a strong dependency on the Indian Prickly Ash tree (Zanthoxylum rhetsa).
life_span: The entire life cycle from egg to adult takes approximately 40 to 50 days. The adult butterfly typically lives for about 2 to 3 weeks.
conservation_status: Threatened / Locally Endangered (Faces habitat pressure; heavily protected in local reserves).

description: The Malabar Rose (Pachliopta pandiyana) is a striking swallowtail butterfly endemic to the Western Ghats of India. Closely related and visually similar to the Crimson Rose, it is distinguished by the much larger white patches on its forewings and the deeper, richer black of its background coloration. The hindwings feature a series of brilliant crimson spots that serve as a strong aposematic warning to predators, advertising the toxicity it acquires from its host plants during the larval stage. With a wingspan of 90 to 110 mm, it is a magnificent sight in the forest canopy. It typically flies higher than the Crimson Rose and has a graceful, slow, and gliding flight pattern. The Malabar Rose is particularly fond of dense, shaded evergreen forests and is rarely seen in open country. It frequently visits flowers such as Lantana and Clerodendrum for nectar, often fluttering its wings while feeding.
habitat: Wet evergreen and semi-evergreen forests of the Western Ghats, typically at elevations between 300 and 1,000 meters.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from flowers like Lantana and Clerodendrum. Caterpillars feed primarily on the leaves of Thottea siliquosa and other Aristolochiaceae plants.
life_span: The life cycle takes about 40 days; adults live for roughly 2 to 3 weeks.
conservation_status: Least Concern (but regionally restricted as an endemic species).

description: The Malabar Raven (Papilio dravidarum) is a distinct and elegant swallowtail butterfly endemic to the Western Ghats of India. It has a wingspan of about 80 to 100 mm. Unlike the highly vibrant birdwings and peacocks, the Malabar Raven features a more subdued but beautifully contrasting palette. Both sexes are predominantly brownish-black. The forewings have a marginal series of white spots, while the hindwings display prominent, elongated white discal spots and a row of smaller submarginal white crescents. A defining characteristic is the complete absence of tails on the hindwings. Interestingly, this species is a Batesian mimic of the toxic and distasteful Common Crow (Euploea core) butterfly. It gains protection from predators by successfully copying both the appearance and the slow, deliberate flight pattern of the Common Crow. It is usually found flying low in the shade of dense forests rather than out in the open.
habitat: Shady understory of wet evergreen and semi-evergreen forests in the Western Ghats, avoiding heavily sunlit areas.
diet: Adults visit flowers of undergrowth shrubs like Lantana and Acanthaceae species. Caterpillars feed on Rutaceae plants, notably Glycosmis pentaphylla.
life_span: The entire life cycle takes around 35 to 45 days. The adult lifespan is approximately 2 to 3 weeks.

description: The Common Jay (Graphium doson) is a fast-flying, restless swallowtail butterfly widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia. It boasts a wingspan of 70 to 80 mm. The butterfly's upper wings are predominantly black, extensively marked with a beautiful, semi-transparent pale blue or greenish-blue central band and a series of matching marginal spots. The underside is more intricately patterned with a mix of silvery-blue, black, and distinct red spots near the base of the hindwing. The Common Jay is famous for its rapid, darting flight, often seen rapidly moving from flower to flower, barely settling long enough to feed. It is a highly active species, particularly during the hot hours of the day. Males of the species are well known for their enthusiastic mud-puddling behavior, often congregating in large numbers on damp sand, riverbanks, or mineral-rich soil to absorb vital salts and nutrients.
habitat: Thrives in a wide range of habitats including deciduous and semi-evergreen forests, urban parks, and gardens, especially where host plants are cultivated.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from a variety of flowers. Caterpillars primarily feed on trees in the Annonaceae family, including the False Ashoka (Polyalthia longifolia).
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes around 30 to 40 days, with adults living for about 2 weeks.

description: The Great Orange Tip (Hebomoia glaucippe) is the largest pierid (white and yellow family) butterfly found in Asia, boasting an impressive wingspan of 80 to 100 mm. The butterfly derives its name from the magnificent, fiery orange patches covering the apical half of its stark white forewings. These striking orange areas are bordered by black and are particularly vivid in males. The undersides of the wings, however, serve a completely different purpose: they are heavily mottled with brown and grey, providing excellent camouflage that perfectly mimics a dead leaf when the butterfly is resting with its wings closed. This butterfly is a very strong and swift flier, often seen soaring powerfully above the forest canopy or hurtling along forest paths. They rarely rest near the ground unless they are mud-puddling, a behavior males frequently engage in to obtain minerals.
habitat: Favors lowland rainforests, dry deciduous forests, and forest edges, often venturing out into open clearings.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from flowers like Lantana. Caterpillars feed on plants in the Capparaceae family, particularly species of Capparis (caper shrubs) and Crataeva.
life_span: The life cycle is relatively rapid, completing in about a month. Adults live for roughly 2 to 3 weeks.

description: The Common Albatross (Appias albina) is a medium-sized butterfly belonging to the family Pieridae, recognized for its brilliant white coloration and swift, erratic flight. Males are predominantly a pure, shining white on the upperside with very narrow black borders, while females exhibit more significant variation, often presenting broader black margins and sometimes possessing a yellowish hue. They have a wingspan ranging from 55 to 75 mm. The Common Albatross is a highly migratory species, often undertaking massive, spectacular flights involving millions of individuals across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia before the onset of the monsoon. Males are avid mud-puddlers and can frequently be observed gathering in huge, dense swarms along damp riverbeds and forest streams to absorb moisture and essential salts. They are energetic insects, constantly on the move, and typically fly close to the ground.
habitat: Forest paths, clearings, and riverbanks in deciduous and evergreen forests. Often found in massive numbers near water sources during dry seasons.
diet: Adults feed on floral nectar. The caterpillars rely on plants of the family Putranjivaceae, specifically species like Drypetes.
life_span: Completes its life cycle in 3 to 4 weeks, with adults generally surviving for about 2 weeks.

description: The Dark Wanderer (Pareronia ceylanica) is an intriguing butterfly belonging to the Pieridae family, notable for its striking sexual dimorphism and impressive mimicry. The males are a beautiful, delicate pale blue with dark veins and black margins, reminiscent of other 'wanderer' butterflies. However, it is the female that truly captivates: she is a masterful Batesian mimic of the toxic Glassy Tiger (Parantica aglea) and other Danainae butterflies. The female's wings are patterned with dark brown and prominent bluish-white streaks, allowing her to perfectly blend in with the distasteful tiger butterflies and thus gain protection from avian predators. With a wingspan of 65 to 80 mm, they have a slow, fluttering flight that further enhances the female's mimicry. They are generally shy butterflies, preferring the dappled shade of the forest undergrowth to open, sunlit areas.
habitat: Prefers the understory and edges of evergreen and semi-evergreen forests, often staying within areas of dappled sunlight.
diet: Adults feed on nectar. The caterpillars primarily feed on the leaves of Capparis species (caper bushes) in the family Capparaceae.
life_span: The entire life cycle takes roughly 30 to 40 days, with the adult butterfly living for approximately 2 to 3 weeks.

description: The Common Gull (Cepora nerissa) is a medium-sized, highly variable butterfly found abundantly across South and Southeast Asia. Sporting a wingspan of 50 to 65 mm, its appearance changes significantly between the wet and dry seasons. Generally, the upper wings are white with heavy black veining and thick black borders, particularly pronounced in the wet season forms. The undersides are typically a mix of yellow, white, and prominent dark veins. The Common Gull is an incredibly active and common sight in open landscapes, gardens, and scrublands. It has a relatively strong and fast flight, frequently visiting low-growing flowers to feed on nectar. The males are also known to congregate in large numbers at damp patches of soil or along riverbanks to engage in mud-puddling. It is one of the most familiar 'white' butterflies encountered in its range.
habitat: Highly adaptable; found in plains, scrublands, dry deciduous forests, agricultural fields, and urban gardens.
diet: Adults take nectar from a wide variety of weeds and garden flowers. Caterpillars feed on plants in the Capparaceae family, particularly Capparis zeylanica.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes about 3 to 4 weeks. Adult lifespan is roughly 2 weeks.

description: The Common Jezebel (Delias eucharis) is one of the most familiar and brightly colored butterflies in South Asia. While the upperside of its wings is predominantly white with bold black veins, its true beauty lies on the underside. The hindwing undersides are brilliantly marked with vivid yellow and a marginal row of striking crimson-red spots bordered by black and white. This vivid coloration serves as a classic example of aposematism, warning predators of its distastefulness, which is derived from the toxins in its larval host plants. It has a wingspan of 65 to 85 mm. The Common Jezebel has a slow, fluttering flight and often flies high in the canopy, making the bright colors on its undersides highly visible from below. It is a very common sight in urban areas, parks, and gardens, as well as in forested regions.
habitat: Ubiquitous in areas where mistletoe grows; found in light forests, urban gardens, and wooded areas across the plains and lower hills.
diet: Adults frequently nectar on flowers like Lantana and Eupatorium. Caterpillars feed exclusively on parasitic mistletoes (Loranthus and Dendrophthoe species) growing on trees.
life_span: The life cycle takes about 30 to 40 days, with the adults living for approximately 2 to 3 weeks.

description: The Lemon Emigrant (Catopsilia pomona) is a remarkably common, highly variable, and fast-flying butterfly found throughout Asia and parts of Australia. True to its name, it frequently undertakes massive, spectacular migrations. The butterfly exhibits incredible polymorphism with multiple forms. Generally, males are a pristine, chalky white or pale yellow with very narrow black borders on the forewings. Females are much more variable, ranging from pale yellow to a deep, rich sulphur yellow, often with broader black margins and sometimes displaying dark spots. They have a wingspan of 55 to 80 mm. Lemon Emigrants are energetic insects, rarely resting for long. They fly strongly and erratically, often low to the ground. During migrations, they can be seen moving in continuous streams across the landscape. Males frequently engage in mud-puddling, forming large, fluttering yellow and white patches on damp ground.
habitat: Found in almost every habitat from dense forests to completely open agricultural landscapes and urban parks, thriving wherever its host plants grow.
diet: Adults avidly seek nectar from a variety of flowers. Caterpillars feed voraciously on various species of Cassia and Senna (Fabaceae family).
life_span: Completes its life cycle rapidly, often in just 25 to 30 days. Adults live for roughly 2 weeks.

description: The Malabar Tree Nymph (Idea malabarica) is a large, breathtaking butterfly endemic to the Western Ghats of India. It is famous for its slow, floating, and exceptionally graceful flight, often appearing like a piece of tissue paper drifting on the breeze. It has a massive wingspan ranging from 120 to 160 mm. The wings are a translucent, silvery-white, elegantly adorned with intricate black veins and large black spots. The butterfly’s weak flight allows it to be carried by wind currents over the forest canopy. Despite its delicate appearance, it is a tough butterfly; its body is leathery and resistant to pinching by predators. Furthermore, it is highly unpalatable to birds and other predators, having sequestered toxins from its host plants during its larval stage. The Malabar Tree Nymph is a true symbol of the pristine rainforests of southern India.
habitat: Restricted to the dense, high-canopy evergreen and semi-evergreen forests of the Western Ghats, typically near streams and deep valleys.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from forest canopy flowers. Caterpillars feed on the leaves of Aganosma cymosa and Parsonsia alboflavescens (Apocynaceae family).
life_span: The entire life cycle takes around 45 to 60 days. Adults can live for several weeks, significantly longer than many smaller butterflies.
conservation_status: Near Threatened (Dependent on pristine evergreen forest habitats).

description: The Blue Oakleaf (Kallima horsfieldii) is widely celebrated as one of the animal kingdom's greatest masters of camouflage. Endemic to the Western Ghats and central India, this butterfly boasts a wingspan of 85 to 110 mm. When its wings are open, it reveals a dazzling upperside of deep blue across the hindwings and a broad, striking orange band across the dark forewings. However, when the butterfly closes its wings and rests on a branch, it undergoes a magical transformation. The underside perfectly mimics a dry, dead leaf, complete with a dark 'midrib' line, 'veins,' and even markings that resemble mold spots or insect damage. The mimicry is so flawless that when perfectly still, the butterfly becomes virtually invisible against a background of dry foliage. It is a strong flier and is often attracted to rotting fruit and tree sap rather than flowers.
habitat: Frequents moist deciduous forests, evergreen forests, and secondary growth areas, usually staying within the thick undergrowth or middle canopy.
diet: Adults rarely visit flowers, strongly preferring overripe or rotting fruit, tree sap, and animal dung. Caterpillars feed on plants of the Acanthaceae family, such as Strobilanthes.
life_span: The life cycle from egg to adult takes about 35 to 45 days. The adults can live for several weeks.

description: The Clipper (Parthenos sylvia) is a large, powerful, and strikingly patterned butterfly found in the forested regions of South and Southeast Asia. It has a substantial wingspan of 100 to 130 mm. The Clipper is instantly recognizable by the broad, semi-transparent white or pale yellow spots forming a jagged band across its forewings. The rest of the wings are deeply colored, typically a rich, metallic green or brownish-bronze with heavy black markings. The butterfly is renowned for its distinctive flight pattern: it executes a few rapid, powerful wing beats followed by a long, elegant glide with its wings held perfectly horizontal. It is highly territorial, with males often perching on high leaves, keeping a watchful eye over their domain, and fiercely chasing away any intruders. It is a prominent and magnificent resident of the jungle canopy.
habitat: Thrives in moist deciduous and evergreen forests, frequently seen near forest clearings, riversides, and deeply wooded paths.
diet: Adults visit flowers occasionally but are more strongly attracted to rotting fruit, tree sap, and wet mud. Caterpillars feed on various climbers in the Passifloraceae and Menispermaceae families.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes approximately 40 to 50 days, with the adult butterfly living for about 2 to 3 weeks.

description: The Tamil Lacewing (Cethosia nietneri) is a stunningly intricate butterfly endemic to the Western Ghats of India and Sri Lanka. With a wingspan of 75 to 95 mm, it is a visual masterpiece. The upper wings exhibit a striking contrast of deep black with a broad, jagged white band across the forewing, while the basal areas are often a vibrant reddish-orange. However, the true beauty lies on the underside, which is completely covered in a complex, mesmerizing pattern of white, red, yellow, and black lace-like lines and scalloped edges. This extravagant patterning serves as an aposematic warning to predators that the butterfly is toxic, having absorbed protective chemicals from its host plants during its larval stage. It has a relatively slow, floating flight and is frequently seen fluttering around its host plants in the forest understory.
habitat: Prefers the edges of moist deciduous and semi-evergreen forests, particularly where passionflower vines are abundant in the undergrowth.
diet: Adults frequently feed on nectar from Lantana and other forest flowers. Caterpillars are highly specialized, feeding exclusively on toxic Passiflora (passionflower) vines, such as Passiflora subpeltata.
life_span: The entire life cycle takes about 35 to 40 days. Adults live for roughly 2 weeks.

description: The Tamil Yeoman (Cirrochroa thais) is a fast-flying, warmly colored butterfly that holds the prestigious title of being the official state butterfly of Tamil Nadu. It has a wingspan of 60 to 75 mm. The butterfly’s upperside is a rich, tawny-orange with narrow dark brown margins and a distinct, dark zigzag line running parallel to the edge of the wings. The underside is paler and crossed by a distinctive silvery-white band. The Tamil Yeoman is known for its rapid, darting flight and its tendency to suddenly settle on leaves with its wings spread flat, basking in the sun. It is a very active butterfly, especially during the brighter parts of the day. Males are frequently observed congregating to mud-puddle on wet soil or animal dung to absorb essential minerals.
habitat: Inhabits moist deciduous forests, semi-evergreen forests, and thickly wooded areas, often found along forest paths and streamsides.
diet: Adults feed on floral nectar. Caterpillars primarily feed on trees in the Flacourtiaceae family, such as Hydnocarpus wightiana.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes approximately 30 to 40 days. The adult lifespan is around 2 weeks.

description: The Dark Blue Tiger (Tirumala septentrionis) is a large, striking butterfly of the Danainae subfamily, known for its deep colors and migratory behavior. It has a wingspan of 80 to 105 mm. The wings are predominantly black or very dark brown, densely patterned with pale, semi-transparent bluish-white streaks and spots. Compared to the closely related Blue Tiger (Tirumala limniace), the Dark Blue Tiger has narrower blue markings, giving it an overall darker appearance. Like other 'tiger' butterflies, it is highly unpalatable to predators due to toxins accumulated from its host plants. Its slow, confident, and leathery flight is characteristic of toxic species, brazenly flying in the open without fear of predation. It is a famous migrant in southern India, moving in massive numbers between the Western Ghats and the eastern plains in response to the monsoons.
habitat: Highly adaptable, found in forests, scrublands, and urban areas, but prefers wooded terrain during its non-migratory periods.
diet: Adults are avid nectar feeders, particularly attracted to Crotalaria plants. Caterpillars feed on various toxic milkweeds and climbing vines in the Apocynaceae family.
life_span: The life cycle takes about 30 to 40 days. Adults can live for several weeks, often surviving long migratory journeys.

description: The Nilgiri Tiger (Parantica nilgiriensis) is a rare and ecologically significant butterfly that is strictly endemic to the high-elevation shola forests of the Western Ghats in southern India. It has a wingspan of 70 to 85 mm. It visually resembles the more common Glassy Tiger but is distinctly smaller, and its wings have much broader black markings, reducing the pale, semi-transparent bluish-white streaks to a much darker overall appearance. Because it is adapted to cool, montane climates, it has a slow, fluttering flight and is often found close to the ground, flying among the dense undergrowth of the shola forests. Due to its highly restricted geographical range and specific habitat requirements, the Nilgiri Tiger is a species of significant conservation concern, highly vulnerable to habitat fragmentation and climate change.
habitat: Restricted entirely to the high-altitude montane evergreen forests (sholas) of the Nilgiri and Anamalai hills, typically above 1,500 meters.
diet: Adults feed on nectar from mountain flowers. Caterpillars feed on specific vines in the Apocynaceae family that grow in the shola forests.
life_span: The entire life cycle takes about 40 to 50 days, with the adults living for approximately 2 to 3 weeks.
conservation_status: Near Threatened (Highly restricted to vulnerable high-altitude habitats).

description: The Common Evening Brown (Melanitis leda) is a highly camouflaged, crepuscular butterfly found across Africa, Asia, and Australia. It is famously active only during the dawn and dusk hours, spending the daytime hidden in deep shade. It has a wingspan of 60 to 80 mm. The butterfly is a prime example of seasonal polyphenism. The wet-season form is dark brown with prominent, large eyespots (ocelli) on the undersides of the wings, which help deflect predator attacks. In stark contrast, the dry-season form lacks these eyespots; instead, the underside perfectly resembles a dry, dead leaf, complete with remarkable variations mimicking fungus, veins, and decay. When startled during the day, it flies erratically for a short distance before diving back into the undergrowth and instantly vanishing into the leaf litter.
habitat: Extremely adaptable. Found in leaf litter and undergrowth in forests, plantations, urban parks, and grasslands anywhere its host grasses are present.
diet: Adults almost never visit flowers; they feed primarily on tree sap, rotting fruit, and fermenting organic matter. Caterpillars feed on various types of grasses and bamboo.
life_span: The life cycle takes about 30 to 40 days. The adult lifespan is roughly 2 to 3 weeks.

description: Burmeister's Stag Beetle (Odontolabis burmeisteri) is a spectacularly large and intimidating stag beetle native to the Western Ghats of India. It is one of the most striking beetle species in the region, with large males reaching lengths of over 90 mm, including their formidable mandibles. The beetle possesses a highly polished, robust body with contrasting coloration: the head and pronotum are a deep, glossy black, while the elytra (wing covers) are a bright, golden-yellow or orange-brown with a distinct black wedge running down the center. The males are famously equipped with massive, elongated, and heavily toothed mandibles used exclusively for grappling and wrestling with rival males for access to females or prime feeding sites. Despite their fearsome appearance, these beetles are harmless to humans and are primarily active at night.
habitat: Montane and sub-montane evergreen forests in the Western Ghats, highly dependent on old-growth forests with plenty of dead and rotting timber.
diet: Adults feed on sweet tree sap and fermenting fruit. The large, grub-like larvae feed on decaying wood and rotting logs in the forest.
life_span: The larval stage is very long, taking 1 to 2 years to develop in rotting wood. The adult beetle lives for roughly 2 to 4 months.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Faces pressure from habitat loss and the illegal pet trade).

description: The Cuvera Stag Beetle (Odontolabis cuvera) is a large, beautifully colored stag beetle distributed across the Himalayan region and Southeast Asia. It boasts an impressive size, with males often reaching 70 to 80 mm in length. The beetle is easily recognized by its striking bicolored appearance. The head, massive mandibles, and pronotum are a deep, heavily pitted black, while the elytra (hardened wing covers) are a vibrant, glossy orange or reddish-brown, bordered by a sharp black margin. The males exhibit extreme polymorphism in their mandibles, ranging from short, scissor-like jaws to huge, sweeping, antler-like pincers used for combat during the mating season. They are primarily nocturnal and are heavily attracted to light. The Cuvera Stag Beetle is a crucial component of the forest ecosystem, as its wood-boring larvae aid in the decomposition of dead timber.
habitat: Cool, high-altitude broadleaf and mixed forests in the Himalayas and surrounding mountain ranges, relying heavily on fallen logs and decaying trees.
diet: Adults primarily feed on tree sap flowing from wounded bark. Larvae feed internally on the decaying wood of various hardwood trees.
life_span: The larval stage can take 1 to 2 years to complete. The adult stage is relatively short, lasting for a few months during the wet season.

description: The Giraffe Stag Beetle (Prosopocoilus giraffa) holds the impressive title of being the world's largest stag beetle. Native to various parts of Asia, including India, large males can reach staggering lengths of up to 120 mm (nearly 5 inches), with their enormous jaws accounting for almost half of that length. The beetle is entirely jet black with an elongated, somewhat flattened body. The defining feature of the male is its extraordinarily long, saw-toothed mandibles, which resemble the long neck of a giraffe, giving the insect its common name. These massive jaws are surprisingly lightweight and are used specifically for lifting and tossing rival males off tree branches during fierce territorial battles. Despite their monstrous appearance, they are gentle giants towards humans. They are nocturnal insects and are strongly drawn to tree sap.
habitat: Tropical rainforests and mature deciduous forests, requiring a constant supply of massive, decaying hardwood trees for larval development.
diet: Adults feed on tree sap and overripe fruits. The large larvae develop inside and consume decaying hardwood logs.
life_span: Larvae take roughly 12 to 18 months to mature in rotting wood. Adults typically live for 3 to 6 months.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Populations are impacted by the destruction of old-growth forests).

description: Davison's Stag Beetle (Hexarthrius davisoni) is a striking and relatively large species of stag beetle found primarily in the montane forests of southern India. The males are particularly notable for their distinctive mandibles, which are elongated, downward-curving, and bifurcated (forked) near the tips, giving them a somewhat antler-like appearance. The beetle has a robust, heavily armored body that is typically a deep, glossy dark reddish-brown to black. These impressive mandibles are used primarily for wrestling other males to secure mating opportunities or territory on prime feeding sites. The females are much smaller, lacking the elaborate jaws, but have stronger, shorter mandibles used for digging into rotting wood to lay eggs. They are primarily nocturnal and can occasionally be drawn to artificial lights.
habitat: Montane evergreen and semi-evergreen forests, often found on or near decaying logs.
diet: Adults feed on tree sap and rotting fruits. The larvae are xylophagous, feeding on the decaying wood of broadleaf trees.
life_span: Larvae take roughly 1 to 2 years to develop in rotting logs. The adults live for approximately 3 to 5 months.

description: Cantor's Stag Beetle (Lucanus cantori) is a prominent species of stag beetle found in the Himalayan region and parts of Northeast India. Males exhibit the classic stag beetle appearance, featuring large, impressive mandibles that are lined with small, sharp denticles (teeth) along the inner edges. The beetle's body is typically a matte to slightly glossy reddish-brown or dark brown, often covered in very fine, short golden hairs (pubescence) on the underside and legs. The exaggerated jaws of the male are primarily weapons for combat, used to flip rival males off branches during disputes over females or sap flows. Like most stag beetles, they exhibit significant size variation depending on the nutritional quality of the wood they consumed as larvae. Females are smaller with short, powerful jaws built for excavating egg-laying sites in dead wood.
habitat: High-altitude broadleaf forests and temperate mountainous regions, dependent on dead and decaying timber.
diet: Adults primarily consume tree sap from wounded bark. Larvae feed internally on the moist, decaying wood of deciduous trees.
life_span: The larval stage can last 1 to 2 years. Adult lifespan is relatively short, spanning 1 to 3 months.

description: The Giant Stag Beetle (Serrognathus titanus, formerly Dorcus titanus) is one of the most famous and widely distributed large stag beetles in Asia. It is a massive, heavily built insect, completely jet-black, and characterized by a flattened, extremely broad body profile. Large males possess formidable, relatively straight, and thick mandibles that are heavily toothed along the inner margin. These jaws are incredibly powerful and capable of delivering a painful pinch. Males are highly territorial and aggressive, using their jaws to grapple, crush, or throw opponents. They exhibit extreme polymorphism; 'major' males have enormous jaws, while 'minor' males have much shorter, smaller jaws, a result of varying larval nutrition. They are popular in the pet trade in parts of Asia and are highly prized by collectors.
habitat: Tropical and subtropical broadleaf forests, frequently found near oak and beech trees with sap runs.
diet: Adults feed on tree sap, particularly from oak and related trees. Larvae feed on the white-rotten wood of broadleaf trees.
life_span: Larvae take roughly 1 to 2 years to mature. Adults are exceptionally long-lived for stag beetles, often surviving 1 to 2 years.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Widespread, but locally threatened by over-collection and habitat loss).

description: The Seven-spot Ladybird (Coccinella septempunctata) is arguably the most recognizable and beloved beetle in the world. Characterized by its domed, oval body, it features bright red or orange elytra (wing covers) decorated with exactly seven black spots—three on each side and one shared centrally just behind the head (pronotum). This bright coloration is a classic example of aposematism, warning predators of its foul taste, caused by toxic alkaloids it can bleed from its leg joints when threatened. This ladybird is a voracious predator in both its larval and adult stages, consuming hundreds of aphids daily. Because of this, it is highly valued by farmers and gardeners as a natural biological control agent. Originally native to Europe and Asia, it has been introduced worldwide to help manage agricultural pests.
habitat: Ubiquitous. Found in gardens, agricultural fields, meadows, and forests—anywhere aphids are abundant.
diet: Both adults and larvae are carnivorous, feeding almost exclusively on aphids, scale insects, and occasionally small caterpillars or insect eggs.
life_span: The life cycle from egg to adult takes about 3 to 4 weeks. Adults typically live for 1 to 2 years, often overwintering in sheltered spots.

description: The Six-spotted Zigzag Ladybird (Cheilomenes sexmaculata) is a highly variable and very common ladybird species found throughout Asia. Unlike the classic spotted pattern of many ladybirds, this species usually features striking, zigzagging black bands running across its bright yellow, orange, or reddish elytra. However, it is highly polymorphic; some individuals may have isolated spots, while others can be almost entirely black. It has a glossy, highly domed shape. It is a tiny but incredibly effective predator, playing a crucial role in agricultural ecosystems by keeping pest populations in check. Both the alligator-like larvae and the adult beetles actively hunt for aphids and other soft-bodied insects among crop foliage. They are particularly active during the warmer months and can produce multiple generations in a single year.
habitat: Very adaptable. Commonly found in agricultural fields, urban gardens, scrublands, and forest edges.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds extensively on aphids, mealybugs, whiteflies, and the eggs of various insect pests.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes around 2 to 3 weeks. Adults generally live for several months.

description: The Fungus-eating Ladybird (Illeis cincta) is a small but distinctly unique member of the ladybird family. Instead of the typical bright red and black predatory warning colors, this species is a bright, solid lemon-yellow, often with a white or pale pronotum marked with a couple of small black dots. This coloration corresponds to its highly specialized diet. Unlike its carnivorous cousins, the Fungus-eating Ladybird is mycophagous—it feeds entirely on powdery mildew fungi that grow on the leaves of various plants. You will typically find them grazing on the white fungal mats covering the leaves of squash, cucumbers, or roses. Because they consume plant pathogens rather than insect pests, they are highly beneficial to gardeners and farmers as a form of natural disease control.
habitat: Found on the foliage of plants susceptible to powdery mildew, commonly in gardens, agricultural borders, and moist undergrowth.
diet: Exclusively mycophagous. Both adults and larvae feed on powdery mildew fungi (order Erysiphales) on plant surfaces.
life_span: The life cycle takes roughly 3 to 4 weeks. Adults live for a few months.

description: The 28-spotted Potato Ladybird (Henosepilachna vigintioctopunctata) is a notorious exception to the rule that ladybirds are a gardener's best friend. This species belongs to the Epilachninae subfamily, which are herbivorous. It is easily identified by its duller, almost fuzzy orange or yellowish-brown elytra covered in exactly 28 small, black spots. Both the spiky, yellowish larvae and the adult beetles are voracious agricultural pests. They feed by scraping the soft epidermal tissue from the leaves of their host plants, leaving behind the tougher veins in a characteristic 'skeletonized' pattern. Severe infestations can rapidly defoliate plants, leading to significantly reduced crop yields. It is a major pest of crops in the Solanaceae family, particularly potatoes, eggplants, and tomatoes, causing significant economic damage across Asia and parts of Australia.
habitat: Agricultural fields, vegetable gardens, and any area where solanaceous host plants are cultivated.
diet: Herbivorous. Feeds exclusively on the leaves of plants in the Solanaceae family (potatoes, eggplants, tomatoes, etc.).
life_span: Life cycle completes in about 30 to 40 days. Adults live for roughly 1 to 2 months.

description: The Dissected Ladybird (Propylea dissecta) is a beneficial predatory beetle widely distributed across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It is a relatively small ladybird and is highly polymorphic, meaning its appearance can vary drastically. The most common form features pale yellow or orange elytra heavily marked with thick black bands or blocks, often making the elytra look like they are 'dissected' into separate colored panels. Some forms can be almost entirely black with small pale spots, while others are mostly pale. Despite its varied appearance, it is a highly consistent and effective biological control agent. It actively patrols the stems and leaves of various crops, hunting down soft-bodied pests. Its ability to tolerate a wide range of temperatures makes it an important natural enemy of pests in diverse agricultural systems.
habitat: Found primarily in agricultural landscapes, grasslands, and gardens where prey is abundant.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds primarily on aphids, but will also consume whiteflies, mites, and insect eggs.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes about 3 weeks. Adults generally live for 2 to 3 months.

description: Cardon's Ladybird (Anegleis cardoni) is a relatively small but highly distinctive predatory ladybird commonly found in the Indian subcontinent. It features a moderately domed, oval body. The elytra are typically a creamy yellow, pale orange, or sometimes pale brown, marked with a very specific pattern: a distinct, bold black longitudinal stripe running directly down the center (along the suture where the wing covers meet), and additional black stripes or elongated spots running parallel along the sides. This striped appearance sets it apart from the more typical spotted ladybirds. It is an active predator that helps regulate populations of small, sap-sucking pests on various plants. While perhaps less famous than the Seven-spot, it plays a vital, localized role in maintaining the ecological balance within its habitat.
habitat: Commonly found in scrublands, agricultural borders, and light woodlands, often on shrubs and low-growing vegetation.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds primarily on various species of aphids, scale insects, and sometimes small mealybugs.
life_span: The entire life cycle takes about 3 to 4 weeks, with the adult beetle living for several months.

description: The Mango Tree Borer (Batocera rufomaculata) is a very large and formidable longhorn beetle that is a major agricultural pest across Asia and the Middle East. It has a massive, elongated body reaching 50 to 60 mm in length. The beetle is predominantly a mottled greyish-brown, which provides excellent camouflage against tree bark. Its most striking features are a pair of bright red or orange kidney-shaped spots on its pronotum and extraordinarily long, thick antennae that can easily exceed the length of its body. The adults are nocturnal and occasionally feed on bark, but the true damage is done by the larvae. The massive, pale, grub-like larvae bore deep into the trunks and main branches of living trees, particularly mango and fig trees. Their extensive tunneling cuts off sap flow, severely weakening and often killing mature, fruit-bearing trees.
habitat: Orchards, plantations, and tropical broadleaf forests where host trees are abundant.
diet: Larvae bore into and feed on the inner wood (xylem) of living hardwood trees like Mango, Fig, and Jackfruit. Adults may gnaw on twigs and bark.
life_span: The larval stage is very long, taking 6 months to over a year inside the tree. Adults live for a few months.

description: The Mulberry Longhorn Borer (Apriona germari) is a large, destructive beetle notorious for its impact on the silk and timber industries in Asia. Measuring up to 50 mm in length, it is typically a yellowish-grey or olive-green color, with a robust, elongated body and very long, segmented antennae. It is easily identified by a distinct, rugged, dark-colored band running across the base of its wing covers. The adult beetles cause damage by feeding on the tender bark of young shoots, but the larvae are far more devastating. The larvae bore straight into the heartwood of living trees—particularly Mulberry, but also Poplar and Apple—creating long, straight tunnels that significantly compromise the structural integrity of the tree. This tunneling often leads to branch breakage, reduced fruit or leaf yield, and sometimes the death of the tree.
habitat: Agricultural plantations, particularly mulberry farms, orchards, and temperate to subtropical broadleaf forests.
diet: Larvae feed entirely within the wood of living trees. Adults feed externally on the bark of young twigs.
life_span: The life cycle from egg to adult usually takes one full year, though it can take two years in cooler regions. Adults live for several weeks.

description: The Artocarpus Borer (Olenecamptus bilobus) is a moderately sized, distinctively patterned longhorn beetle found across South and Southeast Asia. It is relatively slender compared to massive borers like Batocera, usually measuring between 15 to 25 mm. It has an attractive appearance: the body is a soft, velvety grey or pale brown, and it features distinct, large white or yellowish circular patches on its elytra, often outlined in black. Its antennae are exceptionally long and fine. This species is economically significant because its larvae are wood borers that specifically target trees in the genus Artocarpus, which includes important food crops like Jackfruit and Breadfruit, as well as various fig species (Ficus). The larvae tunnel just under the bark in the cambium layer before boring deeper to pupate, which can weaken branches and affect fruit production.
habitat: Tropical and subtropical regions, frequently found in orchards, home gardens, and forests with suitable host trees.
diet: Larvae bore into the wood of living and dying trees, primarily Artocarpus (Jackfruit) and Ficus species. Adults may feed on tender bark.
life_span: The entire life cycle typically completes within a year. Adult beetles live for a few months.

description: The Macadamia Borer (Aristobia reticulator) is a striking and highly damaging longhorn beetle native to Southeast Asia and parts of the Indian subcontinent. It is a large, robust insect, visually notable for its intricate, net-like (reticulated) pattern of orange, yellow, and black across its wing covers. Its legs and very long antennae also feature prominent black and yellow bands, and it has distinct tufts of hair on the basal segments of its antennae. It has gained notoriety as a serious agricultural pest, particularly in macadamia nut orchards, though it also attacks litchi and other fruit trees. The adult females chew slits into the bark to lay their eggs. The emerging larvae then bore deeply into the stems and main branches, hollowing out the wood, causing branch dieback, and severely reducing crop yields.
habitat: Fruit orchards, plantations, and native subtropical forests where host plants are present.
diet: Larvae are internal wood borers of living trees (Macadamia, Litchi, etc.). Adults feed on the tender bark of twigs.
life_span: The life cycle usually spans one year. The adults emerge in the rainy season and live for roughly 1 to 2 months.

description: The Mango Branch Borer (Rhytidodera bowringii) is a medium-to-large longhorn beetle that poses a significant threat to mango cultivation in South and Southeast Asia. The beetle has a narrow, elongated, and somewhat cylindrical body, typically a drab, dark brownish-grey, which helps it blend perfectly with tree bark. Its pronotum is distinctly rough and wrinkled (rugose). The primary damage is inflicted by the larvae, which are specialized branch borers. Unlike trunk borers, these grubs tunnel extensively through the center of younger, terminal branches and twigs of mango trees. This internal feeding hollows out the shoots, causing the leaves to wither and the branches to die back completely, a condition often called 'shoot blight'. This directly impacts the tree's ability to flower and produce fruit, leading to major economic losses for farmers.
habitat: Mango orchards, plantations, and residential gardens wherever mango trees are grown.
diet: Larvae bore exclusively into the branches and shoots of mango trees. Adults occasionally feed on mango bark and foliage.
life_span: The life cycle generally takes one year to complete, with adults emerging during the early monsoon season.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Significant pest of mango orchards).

description: The Monkeypod Roundheaded Borer (Xystrocera globosa) is an elongated, distinctively colored longhorn beetle widely distributed across Asia, Africa, and parts of the Pacific. It measures roughly 15 to 30 mm in length. The beetle has a flat, slender profile with a striking color scheme: the central part of the elytra is a pale, metallic bronze or reddish-brown, bordered by bold, metallic blue or greenish-blue stripes running down the sides. It is a major pest of leguminous shade and timber trees, particularly the Monkeypod tree (Samanea saman) and various Acacia species. The females lay eggs in bark crevices. The larvae rapidly bore into the sapwood, creating extensive, meandering galleries that are tightly packed with frass (wood dust). Heavy infestations can completely girdle and kill young trees or severely weaken mature ones.
habitat: Urban avenues, parks, forestry plantations, and tropical broadleaf forests, specifically targeting stressed or weakened leguminous trees.
diet: Larvae bore into the sapwood and heartwood of leguminous trees (Fabaceae family).
life_span: The life cycle can take several months to a year depending on the climate and host. Adults live for a few weeks.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Pest of shade and timber trees).

description: The Cherry Stem Borer (Aeolesthes holosericea) is a large, imposing longhorn beetle found across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It is a robust insect, heavily built, and entirely covered in a dense, very fine, silky, silvery-grey or golden-brown pubescence, which gives it a shimmering, 'holosericeous' (silky) appearance. The males have extremely long antennae, sometimes twice the length of their bodies. It is a polyphagous pest, meaning it attacks a wide variety of hardwood trees, including cherry, apple, sal, and teak. The larvae are massive grubs that bore deep into the heartwood of both living and freshly felled trees. Their large, oval-shaped tunnels severely degrade the quality of commercial timber and can cause fatal structural damage to living fruit and forest trees. It is a highly significant pest in both forestry and horticulture.
habitat: Deciduous forests, timber plantations, and fruit orchards across tropical and subtropical zones.
diet: Larvae are internal wood borers of numerous hardwood species. Adults do not feed significantly.
life_span: The larval stage spans 1 to 2 years inside the wood. Adults are short-lived, surviving only a few weeks to mate and lay eggs.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Major forestry and agricultural pest).

description: The Indian Glenea Borer (Glenea indiana) is a relatively small but brightly patterned longhorn beetle found in India and surrounding regions. Unlike the massive, drab trunk borers, this beetle is diurnal (active during the day) and very colorful. It typically has a black background color heavily marked with bright yellow, orange, or whitish bands and spots across its head, thorax, and elytra. This bold patterning is likely a form of Batesian mimicry, making the harmless beetle look like a stinging wasp or toxic insect to deter predators. The larvae are wood borers, generally targeting smaller branches, twigs, or the stems of various herbaceous plants and smaller shrubs rather than massive timber. They are active fliers and are often found resting on the foliage of their host plants in the bright sunshine.
habitat: Scrublands, forest edges, and light woodlands where host shrubs and undergrowth are plentiful.
diet: Larvae bore into the stems and smaller branches of various shrubs and trees. Adults may feed lightly on leaves and tender bark.
life_span: The life cycle usually completes within a year. Adults are active for a few months during the warmer seasons.

description: The Pied Paddy Skimmer (Neurothemis tullia) is a remarkably striking, small-to-medium-sized dragonfly found commonly across South and Southeast Asia. It exhibits extreme sexual dimorphism. The males are instantly recognizable by their dramatic wing pattern: the basal half of all four wings is a deep, opaque black, bordered by a stark white band, with the wingtips remaining completely transparent. When flying low over the water, they look almost like small, fluttering black and white butterflies. The females and immature males look completely different; they are a dull golden-brown, with wings that are mostly clear but tinted with amber, brown tips, and a dark brown patch near the middle. They are weak, fluttering fliers compared to other dragonflies, usually staying very close to the water surface or resting on emergent grasses in swamps and rice paddies.
habitat: Swamps, marshes, weedy ponds, and very commonly in agricultural wetlands like flooded rice paddies.
diet: Both aquatic nymphs and flying adults are strictly carnivorous, feeding on small insects, particularly mosquitoes, midges, and small flies.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage lasts several months. The adult dragonfly lives for roughly 4 to 6 weeks.

description: The Wandering Glider (Pantala flavescens), also known as the Globe Skimmer, is arguably the most widespread dragonfly species on the planet, found on every continent except Antarctica. It is a medium-sized dragonfly with a predominantly yellowish-orange to golden body and exceptionally broad, transparent hindwings adapted for long-distance gliding. This species is famous for its incredible migratory behavior; it undertakes multi-generational migrations across oceans, following global weather fronts and monsoon winds to find temporary pools of water for breeding. To support this nomadic lifestyle, its life cycle is incredibly rapid; the aquatic nymphs can develop from egg to adult in little more than a month in warm, temporary puddles. You will rarely see a Wandering Glider perched; they spend almost their entire adult lives in the air, feeding on small aerial insects on the wing.
habitat: Ubiquitous. Highly nomadic, they utilize temporary rain pools, flooded fields, and water tanks for breeding.
diet: Strictly carnivorous. Adults feed on small swarming insects like mosquitoes, flies, and winged ants caught mid-air.
life_span: The nymph stage is extremely rapid (30 to 40 days). Adults can live for several weeks, often spending them migrating.

description: The Slender Skimmer (Orthetrum sabina) is a medium-sized, highly adaptable, and incredibly voracious dragonfly found across a vast range from Southern Europe and Africa to Japan and Australia. It has a very distinct appearance: the body is pale greenish-yellow, heavily striped and marked with black. Its most defining physical characteristic is its abdomen, which is swollen at the base, becomes extremely narrow in the middle, and then club-shaped at the end. It is a highly aggressive predator, notorious for preying not only on small insects like mosquitoes but frequently hunting and consuming other dragonflies and damselflies nearly its own size. It does not stay on the wing constantly; it prefers to perch horizontally on sticks or wires, darting out rapidly to snatch prey before returning to its perch to eat.
habitat: Extremely versatile; found near almost any stagnant or slow-moving water, including ponds, lakes, ditches, and urban water features.
diet: Carnivorous and highly predatory. Adults eat a wide range of flying insects, including butterflies, moths, and other dragonflies.
life_span: The nymph stage lasts a few months underwater. Adult lifespan is typically around 4 to 8 weeks.

description: The Granite Ghost (Bradinopyga geminata) is a medium-sized dragonfly famously known for its extraordinary camouflage. Unlike brightly colored skimmers, this species is heavily mottled in shades of grey, black, and white. This specific coloration allows it to blend perfectly against granite rocks, concrete walls, and stone ruins, making it nearly invisible when resting flat against these surfaces. It is highly adapted to arid and rocky environments, frequently utilizing man-made structures like cemented water tanks, wells, and plastered walls as perches and breeding sites. The Granite Ghost is an ambush predator. It waits motionless on a rock, perfectly concealed, and then suddenly darts out with incredible speed to snatch a flying insect before immediately returning to its resting spot. It is highly territorial, with males frequently chasing away intruders from their chosen rocky perches.
habitat: Rocky outcrops, dry riverbeds, and urban environments, especially near concrete water tanks, wells, and stone walls.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds entirely on flying insects, including mosquitoes, flies, and smaller dragonflies.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage lasts a few months. Adults live for approximately 4 to 6 weeks.
conservation_status: Least Concern (Highly adaptable to urban and rocky areas).

description: The Scarlet Skimmer (Crocothemis servilia) is a strikingly vibrant dragonfly and one of the most common species found across Asia and Australia. It is celebrated for its brilliant coloration and extreme sexual dimorphism. Mature males are a breathtaking, uniform blood-red from their eyes to the very tip of their abdomen, often with a slight amber tint at the base of their wings. Females and immature males, in stark contrast, are a dull yellowish-brown or ochre, with a distinct dark line running down the center of their backs. This species is highly visible as it frequently perches conspicuously on the tips of tall grasses, twigs, or aquatic vegetation. Males are aggressively territorial, constantly patrolling the water's edge and engaging in high-speed dogfights with rival males to defend their prime breeding sites.
habitat: Found near almost any stagnant or slow-moving water body, including ponds, lakes, ditches, and paddy fields.
diet: Carnivorous. Preys on a wide variety of small flying insects, notably mosquitoes and midges.
life_span: Aquatic nymphs take roughly 2 to 3 months to develop. Adults typically live for 4 to 6 weeks.

description: The Ground Skimmer (Diplacodes trivialis) is a small, ubiquitous dragonfly found throughout South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Australia. True to its name, it is almost exclusively found resting flat on the ground, bare soil, or low-lying vegetation rather than perching high up like other dragonflies. It exhibits significant color changes as it matures. Immature males and adult females are a pale greenish-yellow with bold black markings across their thorax and abdomen. However, as males age, they develop a powdery, chalky-blue pruinescence that eventually covers their entire body, making them look completely pale blue or grey. They are incredibly active insects, flying low over the ground and frequently settling on footpaths, sandy banks, and open playing fields. Despite their small size, they are fast fliers and efficient predators of tiny ground-dwelling and low-flying insects.
habitat: Open ground, footpaths, sandy riverbanks, lawns, and agricultural fields, often quite far from water.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on small ground-level insects, midges, and mosquitoes.
life_span: The nymph stage lasts a few months. Adults generally survive for 3 to 5 weeks.
conservation_status: Least Concern (Very abundant in disturbed and open habitats).

description: The Crimson Marsh Glider (Trithemis aurora) is an exceptionally beautiful and visually distinct dragonfly widely distributed across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. The mature male is a stunning sight, featuring a vibrant, glowing magenta or deep purplish-red abdomen and thorax, accompanied by a rich amber or golden-brown patch at the base of its transparent wings. The complex venation of the wings is also distinctly red. Females are far less conspicuous, presenting a modest olive-brown or yellowish body with black markings, which helps them blend into the reedy backgrounds where they lay their eggs. Males are highly territorial and can usually be seen aggressively guarding their domains. They prefer to perch horizontally on exposed twigs, dry branches, or aquatic vegetation overlooking the water, quickly darting out to catch prey or chase away rivals.
habitat: Common around ponds, lakes, slow-flowing streams, and marshes, particularly those with abundant emergent vegetation.
diet: Carnivorous. Actively hunts small aerial insects, including mosquitoes, gnats, and flies.
life_span: Nymph development takes a few months underwater. Adult flying lifespan is approximately 4 to 6 weeks.

description: The Coral-tailed Cloud Wing (Tholymis tillarga) is a medium-sized dragonfly renowned for its crepuscular habits—it is primarily active during the twilight hours of dawn and dusk, or on heavily overcast days. When resting during the day, it hangs vertically deep inside bushes or trees. Its most distinctive feature is found on its hindwings: a large, smoky brown patch bordered by a striking, milky-white, opalescent 'cloud' that flashes brilliantly when the insect is in flight. This white cloud is prominent in males but faint or absent in females. The body is a pale brownish-red. The Cloud Wing is a fast, erratic flier, often seen swarming in large numbers over water bodies just as the sun sets, furiously hunting mosquitoes and other twilight-emerging insects before it becomes entirely dark.
habitat: Weedy ponds, lakes, swamps, and surrounding woodlands. Spends the daytime hiding in dense vegetation.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds heavily on crepuscular insects, especially mosquitoes and midges that swarm at dusk.
life_span: Nymph stage takes a few months. Adult lifespan is roughly 4 to 6 weeks.

description: The Long-tailed Duskdarter (Zyxomma petiolatum) is a slender, elusive dragonfly widely distributed across Asia, yet rarely seen due to its highly specialized lifestyle. Like the Cloud Wing, it is strictly crepuscular, emerging from deep shade only during the very late evening or early dawn to hunt and mate. The dragonfly is uniquely shaped, characterized by an exceptionally long, thin, and needle-like abdomen that swells slightly at the base. Its body is a uniform, dull brown, allowing it to remain perfectly camouflaged while resting vertically among dry twigs and shadows during the day. It has a fast, weaving, and ghost-like flight pattern, skimming just inches above the surface of ponds and slow rivers in the fading light. Because of its dark coloration and twilight activity, it is incredibly difficult to observe and photograph in the wild.
habitat: Slow-moving rivers, heavily shaded ponds, and surrounding dense forests or thickets where they hide during daylight.
diet: Carnivorous. Specializes in hunting small insects active at dusk, primarily mosquitoes and midges.
life_span: Aquatic nymphs live for several months. Adults live for approximately 3 to 5 weeks.

description: The Common Picture Wing (Rhyothemis variegata) is one of the most visually stunning and easily recognizable dragonflies in Asia. Unlike typical dragonflies with clear wings, all four wings of the Common Picture Wing are heavily tinted and beautifully patterned with large, irregular patches of rich golden-yellow, dark brown, and black. It closely resembles a butterfly in both appearance and behavior. Instead of the powerful, darting flight typical of dragonflies, it has a very weak, fluttering, and dancing flight style, often drifting lazily in the breeze over marshes and weedy ponds. They are highly gregarious and are frequently seen flying together in loose swarms. When they rest, they often perch on the very tips of tall reeds with their strikingly patterned wings held wide open, making for a spectacular display.
habitat: Marshes, swamps, heavily vegetated ponds, and slow-moving streams, preferring areas with abundant tall reeds.
diet: Carnivorous. Captures very small, slow-flying insects like midges and gnats mid-air.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage spans a few months. Adults live for around 4 to 6 weeks.

description: The Black Marsh Trotter (Tramea limbata) is a large, powerful dragonfly found across Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. It is a robust insect with a blood-red to dark crimson body, but its defining characteristic is the large, dark, opaque blackish-brown patch situated at the very base of its otherwise transparent hindwings. The forewings are completely clear. It is an incredibly strong and agile flier, frequently observed soaring high in the air, using thermal currents to glide effortlessly for long periods without beating its wings. It rarely lands, spending most of its time patrolling territories or hunting high above the ground. The Black Marsh Trotter is known to be a migratory species, capable of traveling long distances to exploit temporary rain pools and newly flooded marshlands for breeding.
habitat: Open landscapes, marshes, lakes, and temporary rain pools. Frequently seen gliding high above the canopy.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on a wide variety of flying insects caught on the wing, including smaller dragonflies and butterflies.
life_span: Nymphs mature rapidly in temporary pools (1-2 months). Adults live for 4 to 8 weeks.

description: The Greater Crimson Glider (Urothemis signata) is a medium-to-large dragonfly commonly found in the wetlands of South and Southeast Asia. The mature male is a brilliant, deep blood-red across its eyes, thorax, and abdomen, making it a highly conspicuous insect. It closely resembles the Scarlet Skimmer, but can be distinguished by the distinct, dark brown or amber-colored patches at the base of its hindwings, which are heavily veined with red. The females are vastly different, being yellowish-brown with dark markings. Males are intensely territorial and typically perch on prominent twigs, reeds, or lotus leaves emerging directly from the water. From these vantage points, they keep a sharp lookout, aggressively chasing away rival males and darting out to snatch passing insects before returning precisely to the same perch.
habitat: Heavily vegetated ponds, lakes, slow-moving rivers, and wetlands, especially where lotus or water lilies are present.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on a range of aerial insects, particularly mosquitoes, flies, and smaller aquatic insects.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage takes a few months. Adult lifespan is roughly 4 to 6 weeks.

description: The Pygmy Dartlet (Agriocnemis pygmaea) holds the record as one of the smallest damselflies in the world, measuring barely 16 to 20 mm in length. It is a tiny, delicate insect found abundantly across Asia and Australia. Mature males have an apple-green thorax marked with bold black stripes, while the abdomen is predominantly dark, culminating in a strikingly bright reddish-orange tip. The females exhibit remarkable polymorphism; they can be green, red, or even completely orange, often changing color as they age. Due to their incredibly small size and weak flight, they are very easy to overlook. They never fly high, instead fluttering weakly among the dense grasses and low vegetation directly bordering the water's edge, hunting for microscopic insects. They are a vital food source for larger dragonflies and aquatic predators.
habitat: Grassy borders of ponds, marshes, ditches, and paddy fields. Always found very close to the ground.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on microscopic flying insects, tiny midges, and newly hatched mosquitoes.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage takes 1 to 2 months. The fragile adults live for only 2 to 3 weeks.
conservation_status: Least Concern (Extremely abundant in grassy wetlands).

description: The Orange-tailed Marsh Dart (Ceriagrion cerinorubellum) is a highly distinctive and beautifully colored damselfly found across South and Southeast Asia. It is easily recognized by its unique bi-colored abdomen. The base (near the thorax) and the very tip of the abdomen are a bright, vivid orange-red, while the middle segments are a sharply contrasting deep black or dark blue. The head and thorax are typically a pale yellowish-green. Unlike the tiny dartlets, this is a moderately sized, robust damselfly that is quite conspicuous. It prefers to fly among the dense reeds and emergent vegetation of stagnant or slow-moving water bodies. It is a slow flier, frequently perching horizontally on grass stems or floating aquatic leaves. Despite its slow demeanor, it is a highly effective predator of mosquitoes and smaller damselflies.
habitat: Swamps, heavily weeded ponds, slow forest streams, and marshy banks with abundant emergent vegetation.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on small flying insects, midges, mosquitoes, and occasionally smaller damselflies.
life_span: Aquatic nymphs live for a few months. Adults survive for approximately 3 to 4 weeks.

description: The Coromandel Marsh Dart (Ceriagrion coromandelianum) is a vibrant and extremely common damselfly found throughout the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. The mature male is an unmistakable sight: its entire abdomen is a uniform, bright, glowing lemon-yellow or golden-orange, devoid of any dark markings. The thorax is a pale greenish-yellow, and the eyes are olive-green. The females are much duller, typically a pale, earthy brown or dull yellowish-green, allowing them to camouflage effectively while laying eggs. This species is highly adaptable and can tolerate polluted or disturbed water bodies. They fly low among the grasses and are often seen resting in large numbers on the muddy banks of ponds or ditches. During mating, they form a classic 'mating wheel' and fly in tandem while the female deposits eggs into aquatic vegetation.
habitat: Banks of ponds, lakes, ditches, paddy fields, and slow-moving streams, often in fully exposed, sunny areas.
diet: Carnivorous. Hunts small, soft-bodied insects like mosquitoes, flies, and plant bugs.
life_span: Nymphs mature in a few months. The adult flying stage lasts for about 3 to 5 weeks.
conservation_status: Least Concern (Highly abundant and tolerant of pollution).

description: The Blue Grass Dartlet (Pseudagrion microcephalum) is a widespread and common damselfly found across Asia and Australia. It perfectly embodies the classic 'blue damselfly' appearance. The male is brightly colored with an azure blue thorax marked by broad black stripes. Its abdomen is predominantly blue with distinct black rings and 'U' or 'V' shaped black marks on the upper segments. The eyes are a brilliant blue with black caps. Females, in contrast, are generally a muted pale green, dull yellow, or brown, making them harder to spot. They are strong fliers for damselflies and are frequently seen actively patrolling the water surface, skimming over floating algae and aquatic plants. They are fiercely territorial, with males often engaging in rapid, fluttering face-offs to defend their patches of floating vegetation.
habitat: Lakes, ponds, slow rivers, and canals, particularly areas with dense floating aquatic vegetation like algae mats.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on a variety of small aquatic and aerial insects, especially midges and mosquitoes.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage lasts several months. Adults live for approximately 3 to 4 weeks.

description: The Saffron-faced Blue Dart (Pseudagrion rubriceps) is a very common, brightly colored damselfly distributed across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It is easily distinguished from other blue damselflies by the mature male's incredibly striking face: the front of the head and the lower half of the eyes are a bright, glowing saffron-orange. The rest of the body is a classic mix of pale blue and black stripes, with the tip of the abdomen being a solid, bright blue. Females lack the bright orange face and are typically a dull olive-green or brown with black markings. This species is very common along the banks of slow-flowing rivers and streams. Males frequently perch on emergent sticks, rocks, or floating debris, aggressively defending their territory and waiting for females to approach the water for mating.
habitat: Banks of rivers, streams, and large ponds, often preferring slightly moving water over completely stagnant pools.
diet: Carnivorous. Preys on small flying insects, including mosquitoes, gnats, and small flies.
life_span: Aquatic nymphs take a few months to develop. Adults typically survive for 3 to 4 weeks.

description: The Aurora Dartlet (Ischnura aurora) is a tiny, highly migratory damselfly that spans an incredible geographic range from Africa and Asia to the Pacific Islands and Australia. The male is exceptionally beautiful, boasting a pale apple-green thorax marked with black stripes. Its abdomen is a bright, translucent yellow-orange, ending in a striking, vivid blue tip. Like many damselflies, females are polymorphic and exist in different color forms, often transitioning from red to dull brown as they mature. Despite their minute size (barely 20-24 mm) and seemingly fragile build, they are carried by high-altitude winds and are often the first species to colonize newly formed temporary rain pools. They fly very weakly and stay low among the grasses, feeding on microscopic insects. They are a true testament to the resilience of insect migration.
habitat: Grassy edges of almost any water body, including temporary puddles, marshes, ponds, and rice paddies.
diet: Carnivorous. Hunts very small insects, particularly tiny midges, gnats, and mosquito larvae near the water surface.
life_span: Nymph development is rapid (1-2 months). Adults live for 2 to 3 weeks.
conservation_status: Least Concern (Global distribution and highly migratory).

description: The Senegal Golden Dartlet (Ischnura senegalensis) is one of the most widely distributed and common damselflies in the Old World, found across Africa, the Middle East, and Asia. It is a highly resilient species, capable of thriving in highly polluted, brackish, or disturbed waters where other species perish. The mature male has a bright green or yellowish-green thorax with broad black stripes. The abdomen is mostly black, save for a prominent, bright blue band near the tip (the 8th segment). The females are famously polymorphic, with some forms directly mimicking the coloration of the males (andromorphs) to avoid excessive mating harassment, while others are a dull orange, brown, or olive. They are a ubiquitous sight in urban wetlands and are highly active, constantly darting among the low vegetation.
habitat: Virtually any wetland, including highly polluted ditches, brackish marshes, urban ponds, and agricultural canals.
diet: Carnivorous. Consumes a wide array of small flying and crawling insects, particularly mosquitoes and plant hoppers.
life_span: Nymphs mature in a few months. Adult lifespan is roughly 3 to 4 weeks.

description: The Pale Dartlet (Aciagrion pallidum) is an incredibly slender, delicate damselfly found primarily in the Indian subcontinent. True to its name, it has a very pale, almost washed-out appearance. The body is exceptionally thin and elongated, typically a very pale brownish-white, cream, or light tan, with minimal dark markings. This pale coloration provides superb camouflage against dry, brown grass. Unusually for damselflies, it is frequently encountered in dry scrublands, grasslands, and forested areas, often far away from any standing water. It has a very weak, fluttering flight, drifting slowly through tall grasses. During the dry season, it aestivates (a period of dormancy similar to hibernation) deep within dry grass clumps to survive the extreme heat and lack of water, emerging fully to breed once the monsoon rains arrive.
habitat: Dry scrublands, grasslands, and open forests, often found far from water during the dry season.
diet: Carnivorous. Feeds on tiny, soft-bodied insects like midges, gnats, and small leafhoppers found in grasses.
life_span: Nymph stage takes a few months. Adults can live for several months, often surviving long dry spells.

description: The Giant Honey Bee (Apis dorsata) is a large, wild honey bee native to South and Southeast Asia. Averaging 17 to 20 mm in length, it is significantly larger than the common Western Honey Bee. It is famous for building massive, single, open-air combs that can stretch up to a meter across. These spectacular nests are typically suspended from the high branches of tall forest trees, overhanging cliffs, or the ledges of high-rise urban buildings. Apis dorsata is highly migratory, frequently abandoning its combs to travel hundreds of kilometers in response to seasonal changes in flower blooms. While they are prolific honey producers and crucial pollinators for the forest ecosystem, they are notoriously aggressive. When a nest is threatened, thousands of bees will attack en masse, relentlessly pursuing the intruder for long distances. They cannot be domesticated.
habitat: Forests, agricultural areas, and urban centers, always building nests high off the ground.
diet: Nectar and pollen from a vast variety of forest canopy trees and agricultural crops.
life_span: Worker bees live for roughly 4 to 8 weeks. Queens can live for a year or more.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Faces threats from habitat loss and destructive wild honey harvesting).

description: The Indian Honey Bee (Apis cerana), also known as the Eastern Honey Bee, is a medium-sized honey bee native to southern and southeastern Asia. It is slightly smaller and generally less yellow than the Western Honey Bee (Apis mellifera), presenting prominent black and yellow abdominal stripes. Unlike the Giant Honey Bee, Apis cerana is a cavity-nesting species, building multiple parallel combs in dark, enclosed spaces like hollow tree trunks, rock crevices, or human-made hives. Because of this nesting behavior, it is the primary species used in traditional Asian apiculture (beekeeping). They are excellent foragers, capable of flying in lower temperatures than other species, and play a massive role in pollinating local agricultural crops and native flora. They have developed unique defenses against predators like giant hornets, including 'heat-balling' the intruder to cook it to death.
habitat: Mixed forests, agricultural lands, orchards, and urban gardens, typically nesting in natural or artificial cavities.
diet: Nectar and pollen from a wide array of flowering plants, trees, and crops.
life_span: Workers live for 4 to 6 weeks. The Queen can live for 1 to 3 years.

description: The Red Dwarf Honey Bee (Apis florea) is one of the smallest species of honey bee in the world, widely distributed across Asia and parts of Africa. It measures only 7 to 10 mm in length. It is easily recognized by its small size and the distinct, bright reddish-brown coloration on the first two segments of its abdomen, contrasting with black and white bands on the rest of the body. Like the Giant Honey Bee, it builds open-air nests, but on a much smaller scale. It constructs a single, small comb (usually the size of a dinner plate) wrapped securely around a small branch of a dense bush, shrub, or low-hanging tree.
