life_span: Unknown (Likely several years as a subterranean nymph, and a few weeks as a flying adult).
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (Newly discovered, likely highly endemic).

description: The Giant Water Bug (Lethocerus indicus), sometimes terrifyingly referred to as a 'toe-biter,' is a massive, heavily armored, aquatic true bug found across South and Southeast Asia. It is one of the largest flying insects in the world, routinely reaching lengths of 8 to 10 cm. It is an olive-brown, flattened insect equipped with massive, powerful, raptorial front legs resembling crab claws, designed for grabbing and holding large prey. It is an apex ambush predator of the aquatic world. It hangs motionless upside down near the water's surface, breathing through a short snorkel on its abdomen. When a fish, frog, or even a small snake swims by, it strikes with lightning speed, grabs the prey, and injects a highly toxic, tissue-liquefying venom through its sharp beak. Its bite is considered one of the most painful insect bites a human can experience.
habitat: Slow-moving rivers, deep ponds, lakes, and flooded rice paddies. They are highly attracted to artificial lights at night.
diet: Strictly carnivorous. Hunts fish, frogs, tadpoles, large aquatic insects, and small snakes.
life_span: The nymph stage takes several months. The adult bug can live for over a year.
conservation_status: Least Concern (Also heavily hunted for human consumption in Southeast Asia).

description: The Lychee Shield Bug (Chrysocoris stollii) is a stunningly beautiful but highly destructive insect belonging to the 'jewel bug' family, found across South and Southeast Asia. It is a medium-sized bug with a perfectly oval, shield-like shape. It is instantly recognizable by its brilliant, highly polished, metallic coloration, which can shift between bright emerald green, golden-green, or striking purplish-blue, typically adorned with several distinct, symmetrical black spots. While it is a visual marvel, it is a severe agricultural pest. Using its sharp, needle-like piercing mouthparts, it swarms in massive numbers to feed on the tender shoots, flowers, and developing fruits of various economically important trees, primarily Lychee, Longan, and Mango. This feeding causes the premature dropping of fruit and significant loss of crop yield.
habitat: Fruit orchards, agricultural plantations, and gardens where specific host trees (Lychee, Mango, Jatropha) are cultivated.
diet: Herbivorous. Uses piercing-sucking mouthparts to extract sap from the fruits and tender shoots of Lychee and Mango trees.
life_span: The entire life cycle from egg to adult takes about 40 to 50 days. Adults live for 1 to 2 months.

description: The Red Cotton Bug (Dysdercus cingulatus) is a highly conspicuous and damaging true bug found widely across Asia and Australia. It is easily identified by its bright, fiery red or deep orange body, contrasted sharply by a prominent black spot on each forewing and white bands across its abdomen. This striking coloration is aposematic, warning predators of its foul taste and toxic nature. It is an incredibly destructive pest to the cotton industry. While they feed on various plants, they specifically target developing cotton bolls. They pierce the boll to feed on the seeds inside, and in doing so, they introduce a specific fungus that permanently stains the valuable white cotton lint an unsellable yellow or brown color, completely ruining the crop's economic value.
habitat: Cotton fields, agricultural lands, and gardens, frequently found in massive, swarming aggregations on host plants.
diet: Herbivorous. Feeds primarily on the seeds of plants in the Malvaceae family (Cotton, Okra, Hibiscus).
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes about 40 days. Adults live for roughly 2 months.

description: The Dentate Stink Bug (Halys dentatus) is a large, common, and highly camouflaged stink bug distributed across the Indian subcontinent and the Middle East. It has a broad, shield-shaped body that is heavily textured, wrinkled, and colored in mottled, bark-like shades of grey, brown, and black. Its name comes from the distinct, sharp, 'dentate' (tooth-like) points or spines on the 'shoulders' (pronotum) of its thorax. This insect is a master of disguise; when resting flat against the rough bark of a tree, it is practically invisible. Like all stink bugs, if threatened or handled, it will instantly release a highly pungent, foul-smelling chemical cocktail from glands on its thorax to deter predators. It is a highly polyphagous pest, using its long rostrum to pierce the bark of numerous trees to suck out the sap.
habitat: Forests, orchards, urban avenues, and agricultural borders. Found almost exclusively resting on tree bark.
diet: Highly polyphagous herbivore. Uses a piercing proboscis to suck sap from the stems and bark of numerous trees.
life_span: The life cycle from egg to adult takes about 40 to 50 days. Adults can live for several months.

description: The Southern Green Stink Bug (Nezara viridula) is one of the most globally distributed and economically damaging agricultural pests in the world, found in tropical and subtropical regions across all continents. It is a medium-sized bug with a classic, broad, shield-shaped body, and is typically a uniform, matte, bright apple-green color, with three small white dots lined up on the front edge of its scutellum (the triangle on its back). It is a highly destructive, polyphagous insect that attacks hundreds of different plant species, showing a strong preference for legumes (beans, soybeans) and developing fruits. They use their needle-like mouthparts to pierce developing seed pods and fruits to suck out the nutrient-rich juices, causing the fruit to shrivel, deform, or drop prematurely. They possess extremely potent defensive stink glands.
habitat: Ubiquitous. Found in massive numbers in agricultural fields, vegetable gardens, orchards, and weed patches.
diet: Highly polyphagous herbivore. Sucks the sap from developing fruits, seeds, and pods of hundreds of plant species.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes roughly 30 to 40 days. Adults live for about 2 months.

description: The Bean Bug (Riptortus pedestris) is a slender, highly active, and significant agricultural pest found across Asia. It belongs to the 'broad-headed bug' family. It has an elongated, somewhat narrow, brownish or black body, featuring a very distinct, bright yellow or white stripe running continuously along the sides of its thorax and abdomen. Its hind legs are highly developed, elongated, and heavily spined, somewhat resembling those of a grasshopper. The nymphs of this species are remarkable; they are perfect morphological and behavioral mimics of aggressive ants, which provides them with excellent protection from predators. The adults, however, are strong, rapid fliers and major pests of leguminous crops. They swarm soybean and cowpea fields, piercing the developing pods to suck out the liquid from the seeds, causing massive crop losses.
habitat: Agricultural fields (specifically soybean and bean crops), pastures, and grassy margins.
diet: Herbivorous. Feeds exclusively on the developing seeds and pods of leguminous plants (soybeans, beans, peas).
life_span: Life cycle completes in about 30 days. Adults live for approximately 1 to 2 months.

description: The Rice Bug (Leptocorisa acuta), also known as the Rice Ear Bug, is a slender, delicate-looking, but utterly devastating pest to rice farmers throughout Asia and Australia. It has an extremely elongated, thin, and fragile-looking body, typically a pale, yellowish-green or light brown, with very long legs and antennae. Its appearance mimics a dry piece of grass perfectly. The Rice Bug causes immense economic damage by targeting the rice plant at its most critical stage: the 'milky stage.' The bugs swarm the rice paddies and use their piercing mouthparts to puncture the developing rice grains while they are still soft and filled with a nutrient-rich, milky liquid. They suck the grains completely dry, leaving behind empty, discolored husks (chaff), utterly destroying the crop yield. They emit a very strong, foul odor when disturbed.
habitat: Strictly associated with tall, damp grasses and, most devastatingly, flooded rice paddies during the grain-filling stage.
diet: Herbivorous. Feeds exclusively on the soft, developing, milky grains of rice and wild grasses.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes roughly 25 to 30 days. Adults live for 2 to 3 months.
conservation_status: Not Evaluated (One of the most severe pests of rice globally).

description: The Lychee Giant Stink Bug (Tessaratoma papillosa) is an enormous, highly destructive, and visually imposing true bug native to Southeast Asia and China. It is a massive insect, easily reaching 3 cm in length, with a broad, flat, heavily armored, shield-like body. It is typically a dull, dusty yellowish-brown or ochre color. Both the nymphs and the adults are major agricultural pests. They aggregate in large numbers on the young, tender shoots and flower panicles of Lychee and Longan trees, inserting their thick, straw-like mouthparts to drain the sap. This causes the massive dieback of flowers and the premature dropping of fruit, devastating orchard yields. They possess a terrifying defense mechanism: if disturbed, they can forcefully spray a highly corrosive, burning chemical liquid from their abdomen, which can severely blind or burn human skin.
habitat: Lychee and Longan orchards, and surrounding broadleaf forests where host trees are present.
diet: Herbivorous. Uses a powerful proboscis to suck sap exclusively from the shoots and flowers of Lychee and Longan trees.
life_span: The entire life cycle takes almost a full year. The adults are exceptionally long-lived, surviving through the winter.

description: The Legume Stink Bug (Cyclopelta siccifolia) is a large, gregarious, and highly conspicuous true bug found across the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It has a broad, relatively flat, oval-shaped body. It is almost entirely a dark, matte, brownish-black, but is distinctly marked with a prominent, bright yellow or orange spot at the very tip of its scutellum (the central triangle on its back), and small yellow spots along its edges. These bugs are famous for their swarming behavior. Hundreds to thousands of adults and nymphs will congregate in massive, dense clusters on the stems and branches of leguminous trees, particularly Pongamia and Erythrina species. They collectively pierce the bark to feed on the sap, causing the branches to wither. If the colony is disturbed, they collectively release an overpowering, nauseating stench.
habitat: Forest edges, urban avenues, and agricultural borders, highly localized to specific host trees.
diet: Herbivorous. Gregariously sucks sap from the stems and branches of specific leguminous trees and vines.
life_span: The life cycle takes several months. Adults typically live for a few months.

description: The Water Scorpion (Laccotrephes griseus) is a remarkable, highly adapted aquatic insect found in shallow, stagnant waters across Asia. Despite its terrifying name, it is a true bug, not a scorpion. It earns its name from its heavily modified, mantis-like front legs designed for grabbing prey, and a long, thin, needle-like tail extending from its abdomen. This 'tail' is actually a highly functional, non-retractable breathing tube (siphon). It has a flat, broad, incredibly drab brown or greyish body, making it look exactly like a dead, submerged leaf. It is an ambush predator. It lies perfectly motionless in the shallow mud or among aquatic weeds, with its breathing tube breaking the surface of the water. When a small fish, tadpole, or insect swims past, it strikes with its front legs and injects paralyzing saliva.
habitat: Shallow, stagnant, muddy, and heavily weeded edges of ponds, lakes, ditches, and slow-moving streams.
diet: Strictly carnivorous ambush predator. Feeds on tadpoles, small fish, mosquito larvae, and other aquatic insects.
life_span: The aquatic nymph stage takes several months. Adults can live for over a year.

description: The Common Water Strider (Gerris spinolae) is a fascinating, highly specialized predatory insect that spends its entire life walking on the surface tension of water, found abundantly across South Asia. It has an extremely slender, dark brown or black elongated body, but its most remarkable feature is its immensely long, thin, widely spread middle and hind legs. The tips of these legs are covered in thousands of microscopic, water-repellent hairs, creating tiny air cushions that allow the insect to literally stand on the water's surface film without sinking. They do not swim; they row across the surface with incredible speed using their middle legs. They are highly sensitive predators. They rest on the surface, feeling for the tiny ripples caused by insects that have accidentally fallen into the water. They instantly dart toward the struggle, grab the drowning insect with their short front legs, and suck it dry.
habitat: The surface of almost any still or slow-moving water body, including ponds, lakes, ditches, and gentle streams.
diet: Carnivorous scavenger and predator. Feeds primarily on terrestrial insects that accidentally fall onto the water's surface.
life_span: Development from egg to adult takes a few weeks. Adults survive for several months.
